# Conroe ISD Board Meeting — 2021-07-20

# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe Independent School District Board of Trustees
### Regular Meeting — July 20, 2021

*These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions.*

---

## Opening
- Meeting called to order at 6:00 PM by President Hubert; quorum confirmed.
- Representative Scott Moore and Ray Sanders led a moment of silence/prayer and the pledges of allegiance.

---

## Item 2A: Patrons Influencing Education (PIE) Award

The district recognized local healthcare partners who provided critical guidance during the COVID-19 pandemic, enabling CISD to keep schools open with the highest percentage of in-person instruction.

**Presenters:** Barbara Robertson (CISD School Nurse of the Year for Texas) and Chris McCord (Assistant Superintendent of Operations)

**Recipients:**
- **Lone Star Family Health Center:** Karen Harwell, CEO
- **Montgomery County Hospital District:** Dr. Robert Dixon (EMS Medical Director), Chief James Campbell (Chief of EMS), Randy Johnson (CEO), Melissa Miller (COO — not present)
- **Montgomery County Public Health District:** Dr. Charles Sims (Public Health Authority), Dr. Saheed Ibrahim (Chief Epidemiologist), Alicia Williams (Public Health Director — not present)
- **Memorial Hermann The Woodlands:** Justin Kendrick (SVP/CEO), Linda Nelson (Director of Business Development & Marketing), Jason Glover (VP of Operations — not present), Kate Willis (Director of Hospital Operations)

Chris McCord highlighted Memorial Hermann's pivotal role in transforming a CISD facility (stadium, natatorium, and parking lot) into a vaccine hub in one week. Recipients received plaques and pecan pies.

---

## Item 2B: Ambassador Award — District Support Staff

**Presenter:** Blake Clock

**Recipients recognized for outstanding service:**
- **Ellen Cooper** — 29 years with CISD, 21 in payroll; helped communicate with employees about leave during COVID
- **Jennifer Cox** — 14 years with CISD, 4 in information systems; created systems for campus technology needs
- **Tracy Peterson** — 10 years with CISD; first point of contact for parents
- **Jessica Villarreal** — Assisted new team members at the administration building
- **Clay Shoeguard** — Nearly 25 years with CISD; technology repair shop's first technician
- **Corporal Brad Palmer** — CISD Police Department since 2010 (not present); trains recruits and ensures school safety
- **Debbie Walters** — 27 years in print shop, 25 full-time

---

## Item 3: Citizens Participation (Public Comments)

**President Hubert read a statement before public comments:** *"Critical race theory is not part of the Conroe ISD curriculum. Conroe ISD's curriculum is and will continue to be based on the state-mandated Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS) that are available from the Texas Education Agency."*

**He also clarified during comments:** The school board and district are **not mandating masks, COVID vaccines, or suggesting any students must receive vaccinations** to attend school.

### Summary of Public Comments (~20 speakers):

**Critical Race Theory / Curriculum Concerns:**
- **Edward Scruggs** — Opposed CRT, citing Dr. Ben Carson's story as an example of success without race-based ideology; warned of strong parental opposition if implemented.
- **Ginger Russell** — Asserted that social-emotional learning (SEL) is a form of CRT; referenced CISD's improvement plan language about cultural/diversity awareness training; questioned district funding of TASA/TASB.
- **Diane Daniels** — Expressed skepticism about the district's denial of CRT; reminded board members of upcoming re-election dates (2022 and 2024).
- **John Mansoowich** (Pastor) — Argued CRT is incompatible with a biblical worldview.
- **Connor Ernest** — Criticized the NAACP's stance; called for transparency in curriculum, budget, and personnel decisions; questioned enforcement of curriculum standards.
- **Edgar Wingo** — Opposed CRT and diversity/equity/inclusion efforts; called for security cameras with audio in classrooms.
- **Madison Djakovic** — As a mother of a mixed-race child, argued CRT teaches hate and undermines children's self-confidence; advocated for anti-bullying campaigns instead.
- **John Boucher** — Opposed CRT/SEL; urged the board to "condemn critical race theory in all of its forms."
- **Kenneth Earnest** — Criticized district administration's attitude toward parents; proposed a public meeting on curriculum and budget; cited Texas Education Code §11.1512(b)(2) regarding community collaboration.

**LGBTQ+ Issues / Board Member Conduct:**
- **Rachel Walker** — Called on board members to vocally support LGBTQ+ students; referenced hate speech and asked the board to "be louder" for queer kids.
- **Jason Rocha** (Founder/CEO, The Woodlands Pride) — Invited all board members and CISD staff to the Woodlands Pride Festival on October 30 at Town Green Park; cited statistics about suicide risk for LGBTQ+ youth from non-accepting homes.
- **Deborah Weitner** — Referenced a specific board member's (Mr. Inman's) public social media comments calling the LGBT community "one of the most vile, hate-filled communities"; invited board to prove they don't share those views by attending Woodlands Pride.
- **Anne-Marie Kennedy** — Requested the board have a table at Woodlands Pride with resources for LGBTQ+ students; emphasized keeping kids alive and connected.
- **Kristen Johnson** — As a parent, expressed dismay at a board member's social media posts; invited board to participate in Woodlands Pride.

**COVID-19 Vaccines / Masks:**
- **Charles Montgomery** — Opposed vaccine mandates and masks for children; cited statistics claiming zero mortality for children without pre-existing conditions; questioned vaccine efficacy.
- **Carrie Bigford** — Criticized CISD's partnership with Kelsey-Seybold for vaccination clinics; argued the flyer was misleading; cited VAERS adverse event data.
- **Kathy Locetta** — Opposed updated CDC guidelines; warned against implementing mask mandates, social distancing, or contact tracing.
- **Dr. Tom Reed** (Foot & Ankle Surgeon) — Opposed mandating or coercing COVID vaccinations, calling them "experimental biological gene therapy"; cited VAERS data and questioned vaccine efficacy.
- **John Wurtz** — Continued Dr. Reed's testimony; cited myocarditis cases, brain damage, and spontaneous abortions allegedly linked to vaccines; opposed mask mandates.
- **Virginia Young** — Criticized vaccine campaigns in schools; alleged violations of the Nuremberg Code; called for a letter to faculty prohibiting pro-vaccine comments to students.
- **Amita Russo** (Nurse/Researcher) — Argued PCR tests are misapplied, masks are harmful, and COVID injections are not true vaccines.
- **Jonathan Houlihan** (Attorney/Veteran) — Warned the board about legal risks of endorsing emergency use authorization vaccines; urged respect for parental rights and individual liberty.
- **Joe Locetta** — Characterized the pandemic as "manufactured"; urged the board not to follow CDC guidelines.
- **Garrett Costello** — Opposed CISD partnering with medical entities to recommend vaccines; argued the school's purpose is education, not medical recommendations.

**Dyslexia:**
- **Kristen Varisco** — Recounted her dyslexic daughter's experience; discussed the failure of HB 3880 in the legislature; hoped CISD would implement SBOE dyslexia handbook changes.
- **Nicole May** — Thanked Trustee Scott Moore for his advocacy; reported CISD is moving forward with proposed changes to dyslexia identification and services; encouraged use of ESR funds for struggling readers.
- **Chris Delauro** — Questioned discrepancy between national (20%), state (5.8%), and CISD (4.8%) dyslexia identification rates; reported difficulties getting IEP/504 accommodations followed; noted dyslexia program stops at 8th grade.

**Other:**
- **Elias Perez** — Defended his daughter, orchestra teacher Alicia Lynn Perez-Gibbs, against racism allegations made by Joseph Liu; called the accusations slanderous.
- **Joseph Liu** — Continued his multi-month complaint about alleged racist treatment of his son by an orchestra teacher at Collins Intermediate; said he has been refused meetings with the teacher.
- **Andy Liu** (Student) — Gave a victim impact statement describing being replaced as concert master two days before a major concert; blamed the teacher, school, and board for inaction.
- **David Burka** — Called for academic excellence and urged the board to "do the right thing."

---

## Item 4: Consent Agenda

**Action:** A board member requested **Item 4B (Amendment to the 2020-2021 Budget)** be removed from the consent agenda for separate discussion.

**Vote to remove Item 4B:** Passed unanimously.

**Vote on remainder of consent agenda:** Passed (voice vote, no opposition noted).

### Item 4B: Amendment to 2020-2021 Budget
**Presented by:** Mr. Rice

- Budget amendment totaling **$12,104,426** in revenues and appropriations
- **General Fund:** $1,000 in activity fund donations
- **Other Funds:** $12,103,426 for new/revised state, federal, and local grant allocations
- The $1,000 general fund portion was described as a formality (donation in/out)

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Item 5: Safe Return to In-Person Instruction Update

**Presenters:** Dr. Hinds and Chris McCord

### Key Points:
- **Instruction will be 100% in-person** for 2021-2022; no online option available
- **No mask mandate** — per GA-36, schools cannot require masks; however, anyone may choose to wear one
- **No vaccine requirement** — vaccines are not mandated or required for attendance
- **Personal responsibility emphasized** as the primary approach
- Basic illness prevention: stay home with fever of 100°+ or new symptoms; hand washing encouraged

### COVID-19 Protocols:
- **Positive test:** Individual should isolate for 10 days, be fever-free for 24 hours (without medication), and diarrhea-free for 24 hours. Students should notify school nurse.
- **COVID-19 is NOT currently on the CDC excluded illness list** — meaning students cannot technically be mandated to stay home solely for a positive test (unlike chickenpox, etc.)
- **Symptoms without positive test:** Should consider isolating, seek medical guidance, consider wearing a face mask
- **Parent notification:** Dashboard on website will continue; the district is "poised and ready" to reinstitute the classroom-level notification system from 2020-2021 if needed
- **Absent students:** Will be treated like any other absence — teachers will provide work, but there will be no online class to log into

### Significant Board Discussion:
- Board members expressed concern about rising hospitalization rates (Montgomery County went from 4 to 40 hospitalizations in one week)
- Questions raised about how parents will be informed if a positive student remains in the classroom (since the district cannot mandate exclusion)
- Dr. Knoll acknowledged the situation is "becoming more concerning" and protocols would be adjusted as needed
- **Trustee clarification exchange:** A trustee explicitly confirmed with staff that there are no slides/plans to segregate students, mandate vaccines, or mandate masks — all confirmed as not being done

### Summer School Results:
- Over 5,000 students served
- Only 1 student positive, 2 employees positive
- No spread observed; very limited mask use

### Other:
- COVID testing to continue through October 1 (5,000+ tests available)
- Enhanced cleaning/sanitizing continues; hand sanitizer available in buildings and buses
- PPE available for all employees by request
- District will scale response up or down based on conditions

---

## Item 6A: House Bill 3 Implementation — Board Adoption of Plans and Goals

**Presenters:** Dr. Upshaw and Mr. Colson

The state requires the district to adopt plans for early childhood literacy, early childhood math, and college/career/military readiness (CCMR).

### Goals (5-year targets, based on "Meets" level — above state passing rate):
1. **Third Grade Reading:** Increase from 52% to 56% at meets level (state passing is "approaches" — 74%)
   - Progress measures: letter sounds, letter ID, reading levels K-2
2. **Third Grade Math:** Increase from 51% to 55% at meets level (state passing is 75%)
   - Progress measures: numeracy, counting sets across grade levels
3. **CCMR:** Increase graduates meeting CCMR from 63% to 76%
   - Sub-population minimum goal: 60% by 2025 (equivalent to "A" in state accountability)
   - Progress measures: TSI passage rates, industry-based certifications, economically disadvantaged graduate CCMR rates

### Board Discussion:
- President Hubert asked about tracking JROTC students into military service; staff acknowledged campuses may have that data but it's not readily available at the district level
- Discussion about JROTC's purpose being broader than military preparation
- Questions about early childhood assessment tools (MASS reading assessment, 3x/year)

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Item 6B: Preliminary 2021 STAAR 3-8 and EOC Results

**Presenters:** Dr. Winkler, Dr. Medford, and Mr. Colson

### Key Findings:
- **CISD administered ~100,000 tests** (only ~2% decrease from 2019 vs. ~13% decrease statewide)
- CISD scores dipped from 2019 but **did not experience the significant decreases seen at the state level**
- Testing a higher percentage of students while having smaller score decreases than the state was viewed as a strong indicator of success
- **End of Course (EOC) exams:** Slight increases in English I and English II; slight regression in Algebra I, Biology, and U.S. History — all less regression than state averages

### Scores presented (2019 comparison to 2021):
- Elementary: 3rd and 4th grade reading, math, and writing
- Middle School: 5th grade reading, math, and science; 6th grade reading and math; 7th grade reading, math, and writing; 8th grade reading, math, science, and social studies
- High School: English I, English II, Algebra I, Biology, U.S. History

### Board Discussion:
- The district did NOT lower expectations or "move the finish line" for students
- Summer school served 5,000+ students — highest in recent memory
- Individual student plans will be in place from Day 1 of the new school year (rather than spending 6 weeks assessing)
- Hurricane-impact studies suggest recovery within approximately one year
- Additional federal funding and tutoring mandates will support recovery efforts

---

## Item 7A: Select Job Order Contracting — Administration Building Office Reconfiguration

**Presenter:** Mr. Foster

- Project to reclaim and reconfigure office space in the administration building after all private tenants moved out
- **Contractor:** Joris Construction (from the JOC vendor pool approved by the board in November 2019)
- **Cost:** $606,826
- JOC method selected as appropriate for this smaller-scale project (vs. CM-at-Risk for larger projects)

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

- Question about building material cost increases — Mr. Foster noted steel prices are a concern but believes they may be near peak; budgets have been tracking with the bond plan so far.

---

## Item 8A: Award of RFP 21-03-09 — Toner and Ink Jet Cartridges

**Presenter:** Rick Reeves

- **Vendors:** Tree House (OEM cartridges) and VQuest Office Machines & Supplies (remanufactured)
- **Annual estimated expenditure:** $450,000
- 18 vendors submitted responses
- Contract through July 2022 with option for two additional one-year renewals (through July 2024)

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Item 8B: Award of RFP 21-04-07 — Medical Stop-Loss Coverage

**Presenter:** Rick Reeves

- **Awarded to:** HM Insurance Group
- **Annual estimated expenditure:** ~$2.5 million
- Mirrors current $550,000 individual stop-loss level with no lasering
- **Increase of ~$69,000 (2.8%)** over current plan
- Previous vendor (UnitedHealthcare, year 2 of contract) proposed a **21%+ increase**
- Contract effective for 2 years through August 31, 2023, with option for 3 additional one-year renewals through August 2026
- Evaluated by financial services, HR, and Gallagher Benefit Services (consultant)

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Item 8C: Financial Reports

**Presenter:** Ms. Garza

### Key Financial Data (as of June 30, 2021):
- **General Fund Total Assets:** $309 million
- **Cash & Investments (General Fund):** $293.7 million ($227.3M in state pools, $53M in long-term investments with TCG)
- **General Fund Revenue YTD:** $536.5 million
- **General Fund Expenses YTD:** $395.7 million
- **2019 Bond:** $272.9 million expended/encumbered; **$378.5 million remaining**
- **Self-Funded Insurance:** June deficit of $367,000 (revenue $4.4M, expenditures $4.7M); YTD gain of $644,000
- **Wellness Center participation:** Up in June to 418 visits
- **Investment Portfolio:** Combined yield of ~18 basis points vs. 90-day T-bill at 0.048

### Board Discussion:
- Question about whether the $644K YTD gain in self-funded insurance would hold through the remaining summer months — staff noted June trends suggest it should be close.
- President Hubert noted the board had **3 budget workshops and 2 formal budget presentations** during the year, all open to the public.

---

## Item 11A: Select Delegate to TASB Delegate Assembly

**Background:** As CISD has grown to become one of the 10 largest districts in Texas, it is now eligible for a seat on the TASB Board of Directors.

**Nomination:** Scott Moore — nominated for TASB Delegate Assembly representative

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Item 11B: Select Representative to TASB Board of Directors

**Nomination:** Scott Moore — nominated for TASB Board of Directors seat

**Note:** This is CISD's **first-ever** seat on the TASB Board of Directors, made possible by the district's growth. The district holds this seat as long as its ADA exceeds 125% of state ADA. Moore will transition from delegate assembly to the board of directors position.

**Vote:** Passed unanimously.

---

## Adjournment

Motion to adjourn passed. Meeting concluded.

---

| Item | Action | Vote |
|------|--------|------|
| Consent Agenda (minus Item 4B) | Approved | Unanimous |
| Budget Amendment (Item 4B) | Approved ($12.1M) | Unanimous |
| HB 3 Goals (Literacy, Math, CCMR) | Approved | Unanimous |
| JOC for Admin Building ($606,826) | Approved | Unanimous |
| Toner/Ink Cartridges RFP ($450K) | Approved | Unanimous |
| Medical Stop-Loss (HM Insurance, ~$2.5M) | Approved | Unanimous |
| TASB Delegate — Scott Moore | Approved | Unanimous |
| TASB Board of Directors — Scott Moore | Approved | Unanimous |

## Notable Items
- **No mask mandate, no vaccine mandate, no segregation of students** — confirmed multiple times by board and administration
- **No online instruction option** for 2021-2022
- **Rising COVID hospitalizations** in Montgomery County acknowledged as concerning; protocols to be adjusted as needed
- **Significant public comment** (~20 speakers) on CRT, LGBTQ+ issues, COVID vaccines/masks, and dyslexia
- Multiple speakers invited board to attend **Woodlands Pride Festival (October 30)** in response to a board member's (Mr. Inman's) public social media comments about the LGBTQ+ community
- Board president's pre-comment statement: **CRT is not part of CISD curriculum; curriculum is based on TEKS**
- **STAAR scores** dipped from 2019 but less than statewide decreases; district tested a higher percentage of students than the state average
- **First-ever TASB Board of Directors seat** for CISD — Scott Moore selected
